<findings>

[observation] [info] High cohesion (0.8) — files are tightly interconnected
[observation] [info] 5 imports flowing to hench-core suggests tools depend on agent types but not vice versa
[observation] [info] Coupling ratio (0.2) reflects necessary dependencies on core types while maintaining separation
[observation] [info] Guard subsystem (commands, paths) provides security boundaries for autonomous execution
[pattern] [info] Tools (files, git, shell) share guard infrastructure but remain individually testable
[relationship] [warning] Tooling imports from core (5) for shared types but core also imports tooling (4) — bidirectional coupling creates potential for circular dependency issues
[anti-pattern] [info] Guard modules (commands.ts, paths.ts) duplicate allowlist patterns that could be consolidated into a single declarative config
[anti-pattern] [warning] tools.ts re-exports individual tool modules but also contains orchestration logic — split into tools/index.ts (exports only) and tools/registry.ts (orchestration)
[suggestion] [warning] Standardize guard modules to use consistent allowlist or denylist pattern for both commands and paths
[suggestion] [info] tools/test-runner.ts was recently moved from agent/ — verify all imports updated and no stale references remain

</findings>

<insights>

- High cohesion (0.8) — files are tightly interconnected
- Provides sandboxed execution environment with command and path guards
- Tools for file operations, git, and shell access with security constraints
- Guard subsystem (commands, paths) enforces sandbox boundaries orthogonal to tool implementations
- Guard modules use allowlist pattern for commands but denylist pattern for paths — inconsistent security model

</insights>
